title: Half Banked: The Economic Impact of Cash Management in the Marijuana Industry
abstract: We investigate the economic value of cash management. In the legal marijuana industry, where only half of businesses have access to cash management services from a financial institution, we examine dispensary profitability using administrative and survey data. Our results show that businesses with cash management charge higher retail prices (8.3%), pay lower wholesale prices (7.3%), and have higher sales volume (19%). Together, these advantages create a 40% increase in profitability. These results support our model in which reputational capital and administrative costs drive profitability regardless of whether national banks, credit unions, or fintech provide the cash management functions.

article.introduction:
Banks create value by servingtwo primary functions: lending and cash management. The empirical literature shows that bank lending generates broad and persistent economic value (Beck, Demirguc-Kunt, and Martinez Peria (2006), Becker (2007), Morse (2011), Adelino, Schoar, and Severino (2015), and Cortés and Strahan (2017)).1The lending function of banks evolved from their earlier cash management function, as banks were originally commodity warehouses (see Donaldson, Piacentino, and Thakor (2018)). This cash management function, whereby banks collect, verify, guarantee, and monitor a firm's cash position to ensure its financial stability, is a fundamental economic service that greases the wheels of the economy. Yet, little is known about the economic value of the cash management function of financial intermediaries, or about what aspects of cash management create value.2
  In this paper, we shed light on the questions above by examining the effect of cash management on firm profitability. We do not ask whether cash management has a positive effect on firms—despite the lack of empirical evidence, its near-ubiquitous use suggests that it does. Instead, we seek to quantify the magnitude of its economic benefits as a stand-alone banking function. There are two major empirical challenges to capturing the effect of cash management on firm profitability. First, almost all firms have access to cash management; those few that do not are outliers. As a result, most settings related to our central question do not provide valid treated or control firms. Second, if firms have banking access, they generally have access to both the lending and the cash management functions. The benefits of cash management, in other words, are inseparable from the benefits of lending. We address these challenges by using a unique empirical setting that allows us to isolate the economic benefits of cash management, namely, the marijuana industry in the United States.
  The U.S. marijuana industry is economically large and rapidly growing.3Firms in this industry face regulatory hurdles, however, in obtaining basic financial services, due to the fact that marijuana is legal in some states but illegal under federal law (Mace, Patel, and Seegert (2020)).4In particular, federal regulations prevent marijuana dispensaries from accessing federal resources. These regulations imply that financial institutions, which are regulated by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (FDIC), are laundering money when they provide banking services to the marijuana industry. Federal guidelines for banks about working with marijuana firms have been erratic, ambiguous, and onerous, and heavy penalties for violations have discouraged banks from serving the marijuana industry. As a result, a substantial fraction of marijuana dispensaries must conduct business on an all-cash basis.
  These bank regulations that limit cash management services shift the supply and demand for marijuana products in wholesale and retail markets. In the wholesale market, operating without cash management increases both counterparty risk and administrative costs by forcing suppliers to securely transport, verify, and store cash—roles that financial institutions typically coordinate. Frictions in the wholesale market affect dispensaries in the retail market. In general, cash management services generate a stream of quasi-rents that increase the value of a firm's reputational capital. Firms can use cash management to build reputational capital, which allows them to command better terms in the wholesale market and offer an implicit quality guarantee to customers in the retail market (Klein and Leffler (1981) and Shapiro (1983)). In our context, this means that dispensaries with access to cash management services can create a “clientele effect” that allows them to sell at a price premium relative to dispensaries without cash management, that is, retail customers assign a lower marginal value to products sold at dispensaries without cash management (Buchak et al. (2018)).
  Motivated by the above insights, we develop a model that links reputational capital, clientele effects, and administrative costs to supply and demand in the wholesale and retail markets. The model predicts that dispensaries with cash management (i) charge higher retail prices, (ii) pay lower wholesale prices, and (iii) buy and sell higher quantities, which lead dispensaries with cash management to have higher profitability. The model further predicts that cash management creates economic value in the wholesale and retail markets by allowing dispensaries to reduce administrative costs and counterparty risk and to provide a quality guarantee to customers.
  To test these predictions empirically, we identify a quasi-experimental setting in which cash management services were randomly assigned to some marijuana dispensaries and not others. Specifically, in Washington state, two credit unions—with a total of 21 branches across the state—decided to offer cash management services to a limited number of marijuana dispensaries. The state used a lottery system to allocate licenses to sell recreational marijuana. These credit unions could accept deposits and provide cash management services to marijuana dispensaries but could not lend them money.5These features of banking in the marijuana industry in Washington state allow us to separate the economic value of cash management from the value of lending.6
  We combine three novel data sets to estimate the causal effects of cash management on the profitability of dispensaries. The first data set comprises administrative records from the Washington State Liquor and Cannabis Board (WSLCB), which cover the universe of 62 million transactions at marijuana dispensaries from July 2014 to February 2017. We use wholesale and retail prices at the product (i.e., strain) level to calculate product-level profitability as the difference between retail and wholesale prices multiplied by quantity. The second data set consists of bank and credit union branch locations, which we obtain from the FDIC and the National Credit Union Administration (NCUA). The third data set comes from a comprehensive survey of marijuana business managers/owners (“owners” hereafter) in several states that we conducted between July 10, 2016 and October 31, 2016. The survey provides information about a dispensary's finances, such as whether they have access to cash management services, as well as about the individuals who run marijuana dispensaries.
  Using the above data, we use an instrumental variables (IV) strategy based on physical proximity to the participating credit unions to estimate the effects of cash management on firm profitability. Research shows that shorter distances between firms and financial institutions allow banks to collect soft information about firms, which facilitates the formation of banking relationships.7Distance should be particularly important in the marijuana industry, where regulations require financial institutions to gather detailed information, perform on-site visits, and file thousands of Suspicious Activity Reports (SARs) merely to hold cash balances. In short, physical proximity allows credit unions to verify that marijuana dispensaries generate cash through legal activity.
  For our instrument to be valid, it must predict access to cash management and be uncorrelated with confounding factors such as a dispensary owner's skill. We find that dispensaries within one mile of a participating credit union have a 28 percentage point higher probability of having cash management services. In contrast, placebo analysis shows that proximity to a bank or alternative credit union does not predict access to cash management. Evidence from our survey data further shows that owner characteristics, including educational attainment, experience, and exposure to entrepreneurship, are not correlated with the instrument, alleviating concerns that these factors, rather than cash management, drive our results.8
  Consistent with our model predictions, we find that dispensaries with cash management pay lower wholesale prices, charge higher retail prices, and sell larger quantities. More specifically, we first find that dispensaries with cash management receive a $0.51, or 7.3% discount, on their wholesale price for a given product (i.e., strain)—the average wholesale price for dispensaries with and without cash management is $6.42 and $6.93, respectively. In addition, dispensaries with cash management charge $1.00 (= $13.04 − $12.04), or 8.3%, more for the same product. Dispensaries with cash management also sell 1.29 (= 8.09 − 6.8) g more per transaction than dispensaries without cash management, that is, they sell a higher-quality product from the perspective of the retail customer.9Taken together, these effects lead dispensaries with cash management to earn $21.06 (= $55.81 − $34.75) more per transaction than dispensaries without cash management.
  We conduct a battery of placebo tests, sensitivity analyses, and alternative empirical methods to test the robustness of our results. We find support for our results using ordinary least squares (OLS), a differences-in-differences design, and different IVs. Moreover, we find that entry and exit decisions had little effect on our findings, as entry was gradual and strictly controlled by the lottery and licensing process, and only two exits occurred over our sample period.
  Turning to the economic value of cash management, we estimate that it is equal to roughly $17,700,000 in the marijuana industry in Washington state over the 31 months spanning July 2014 to February 2017. This amounts to about 1.8% of total industry sales over the sample period. We find that $5,973,076 of the economic value comes from the wholesale market, while the remaining $11,710,372 comes from the retail market. These results suggest that regulations limiting cash management services lead to real economic costs and in turn to substantial loss of economic value.
  A natural question to arise is whether the banking needs of marijuana-related firms differ materially from those of typical firms. While the marijuana industry is unique, the ongoing value of cash management comes from fundamental functions (i.e., transaction processing and warehousing) that extend beyond our setting and beyond the management of physical cash. For example, online banking, cryptocurrencies, and digital warehousing require bookkeeping (e.g., counting currency, tracking cash flows, and recording transactions), safe storage (cybersecurity and information technology support), and currency issuance (providing legal currency to complete transactions). The benefits of the warehousing function for marijuana firms are quite similar to those for firms in most industries, and our model suggests that these benefits hold regardless of whether national banks, credit unions, or fintech provide these functions.
  Our setting allows us to shed light on how credit unions, local and national banks, and fintech services respond to uncertainty, risk, and new growth opportunities. The demand for cash management services is driving an explosion of innovation in the financial services sector, including fintech. We investigate a unique part of this demand in an industry that has limited access to traditional banking services. Traditional banks face regulatory hurdles to working with marijuana businesses. Local credit unions designed customized, small-scale solutions to provide these firms with financial services, and fintech firms developed technology that allowed credit unions to scale up these business models.
  Our paper fits in the intersection of banking, financial development, and economic outcomes. Our results provide early empirical evidence consistent with the model in Donaldson, Piacentino, and Thakor (2018) by demonstrating substantial economic value of cash management for marijuana dispensaries. Our results complement and extend current research on cash management by studying the value of introducing, rather than intensifying, the use of cash management.10Thakor (2020) finds that fintech's greatest impact so far is in payments, clearing, and settlement services. We complement these findings by calculating the value of warehousing and documenting recent fintech innovations for these services. We also add to research on financial exclusion by showing that it negatively affects firm profitability.11Finally, our results provide insights relevant for the future of the banking industry. We show that local financial institutions and fintech played complementary roles to assume some of the functions that have traditionally been filled by large, national financial institutions.
  The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. SectionIprovides institutional background about the marijuana industry. SectionIIintroduces our model of the mechanisms through which cash management can create value for marijuana dispensaries. SectionIIIdescribes our data and SectionIVdiscusses our empirical design. SectionVpresents the empirical results. SectionVIturns to robustness. Finally, SectionVIIconcludes.
article.I. Background:
This section describes the general functions of banking institutions, the legal status of banking in the marijuana industry, and how banking in the marijuana industry works. SectionIVof theInternet Appendixprovides an in-depth review of institutional details in the marijuana industry.12
  Distance and bank size and scope have traditionally played an important role in banks' information gathering and decision making.13Evidence shows that physical proximity is an important determinant of lending relationships (Petersen and Rajan (1994), Petersen and Rajan (2002), Rajan, Seru, and Vig (2015), Nguyen (2019), and Berger (2022)). Proximity to borrowers gives banks a comparative advantage in gathering and using soft information.14
  While this evidence pertains primarily to lending relationships, insights from these studies are relevant to cash management relationships in general (Donaldson, Piacentino, and Thakor (2018)). Specifically, when banks accept deposits, banking regulations require that they verify the business/account holder, determine the source of funds, and file SARs for “abnormal” transactions. Hence, record-keeping and due diligence efforts are important components of a cash management relationship (Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (2002)).
  The Comprehensive Drug Abuse Prevention and Control Act of 1970 includes a schedule with five tiers of controlled substances based on characteristics such as acceptable medical use, the potential for abuse, and general safety. Schedule I substances are defined as drugs with no currently accepted medical use and a high potential for abuse; examples include heroin, LSD, and marijuana (U.S. Drug Enforcement Administration (2002)). As a result, the U.S. government prohibits marijuana production and consumption.
  Individual states, however, have the right to set their laws regarding marijuana under the 10thAmendment of the U.S. Constitution. In 2012, individual states started legalizing recreational marijuana for adults 21 years of age and older. As of 2022, 19 states and Washington DC have legalized recreational marijuana.
  Following state-level marijuana legalization, rules about whether financial institutions could work with the marijuana industry were ambiguous. FDIC-insured financial institutions are subject to federal law and require specific directions from federal regulators before interacting with marijuana firms (FIN-2014-G001 (2014)).
  On August 29, 2013, the FDIC issued guidance on how financial institutions should interact with recreational marijuana dispensaries (Cole (2013)). Additional guidance came from the Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (FinCEN) to clarify Bank Secrecy Act (BSA) expectations. The guidelines suggest that banks “know their clients” to ensure they are not engaged in illegal activities as outlined in the Cole memo (Cole (2013)). Unfortunately, this guidance includes the provision that all rules could be revoked at any time (Cole (2013)). Moreover, the Drug Enforcement Administration formally warned banks that marijuana remains illegal at the federal level, and the CEO and President of Colorado Bankers Association urged its members officially to avoid serving marijuana businesses (Colorado Bankers Association (2022)). Even with this guidance, the fact remains that accepting cash from marijuana firms is regarded as money laundering under federal law.
  As of May 2023, access to cash management is an ongoing struggle for marijuana retailers. Due to the current legal status of marijuana in the United States, less than 4% of all banks and credit unions are willing to provide banking services to marijuana firms (Gurien and Nathan (2021)). Congress has failed to pass concrete laws to encourage banking to this industry, and thus most financial institutions continue to wait on the sidelines. SectionIVof theInternet Appendixprovides a list of proposed banking regulations and policies related to marijuana.
  Most dispensaries have struggled to obtain financial services due to these regulatory ambiguities. For example, based on responses of dispensary owners in Washington state between July 10, 2016 and October 31, 2016 (see SectionIII.Cfor more details about the survey), TableIshows that only 49% of dispensaries have access to a business bank account, 30% do not use any financial services, and 18% resort to using personal bank accounts. Of dispensaries that have business accounts, only 12% have accounts with national banks. And while some survey respondents indicate the use of personal bank accounts, we note that banks frequently close such accounts when they discover that the account is related to a marijuana business. Numerous anecdotes, discussed in SectionIVof theInternet Appendix, corroborate these responses.
  One effect of limited financial services is that financial transactions in the marijuana industry are mostly cash transactions. Based again on our survey of dispensaries in Washington state, TableIIshows that 49% accept cash only, 40% also accept checks, and only 3% accept credit cards. Even those dispensaries with bank accounts have limited payment processing options due to banking regulations. As the size of the marijuana industry is increasing rapidly, cash management is becoming a greater concern (Weed and Julie (2018a)). Indeed, Merz and Riepe (2021) show that marijuana dispensaries report that their major challenges are managing customer payments, making financial business transactions, and accessing financial capital.
  The owners of marijuana dispensaries in our survey worry about the safety and efficiency of an all-cash business (Weed and Julie (2018b)). TableIIfurther shows that among our survey dispensaries, 30% use a security company to transport their cash or product at an average cost of $348 a month. Cash is also more vulnerable to theft, with survey respondents suspecting an average loss from theft of $618 per month. Even normal business activities such as payroll are more costly when conducted in cash. For example, many dispensaries must pay employees in cash. Dispensaries bear the risk of holding payroll in cash, and on payday this risk shifts onto employees. In response to these costs, dispensaries are willing to pay large fees to financial institutions for their services—our survey respondents report paying monthly fees between $437 and $1,059 for access to cash management.
  Theft and crime in the communities in which dispensaries are located have increased as a result of the lack of cash management (Espino, Adan, and Maryam Mirnateghi (2022)). Local financial institutions have therefore faced pressure to provide banking solutions. TableIshows that 69% of the dispensaries with cash management services have obtained such services through a community or regional credit union. In contrast, only 11% of dispensaries report working with a national bank.
  By April 2014, two credit unions in Washington state—Salal and Numerica—announced that they would work with marijuana dispensaries due to public safety concerns. The accounts gave dispensaries access to limited cash management services, which in practical terms meant that these firms could use depository services. However, because taking such deposits was technically considered money laundering under federal law, these credit unions needed to develop careful processes for managing these accounts to ensure they were compliant with federal guidance.
  Salal and Numerica relied on relationships with the local community of financial regulators, business leaders, legal experts, and state regulators to reduce uncertainty and limit the risks of working with recreational marijuana businesses. Together they designed a customized, small-scale framework for providing financial services to marijuana businesses. This solution, which evolved over time, was labor-intensive and hyperlocal, emphasizing due diligence, strict compliance, and constant contact with state regulators. For example, the account application process took between 7 and 10 days and required on-site visits. Account opening procedures included a review of the applicant's business license documentation, criminal background checks, funding and financial scrutiny, and a review of public records from the WSLCB. These accounts required a daunting increase in monitoring, reporting, and special services, which we detail in SectionIVof theInternet Appendix. By October 2014, Salal had received 200 requests for accounts. Despite the intense demand, Salal and Numerica limited the number of new accounts to keep up with reporting requirements.
  These characteristics of the banking sector in Washington state allow us to isolate the effect of cash management from bank lending and other confounding factors, and thus to empirically quantify the economic effect of cash management on the profitability of marijuana firms.
article.II. Model:
Our research question is twofold. First, we seek to estimate the economic impact of cash management on a firm's profitability. Second, we seek to understand what aspects of cash management create value. In this section, we begin by introducing a model of the mechanisms through which cash management can create value by lowering market frictions. This model yields four key predictions, two for the wholesale market and two for the retail market. We next describe institutional details about the marijuana market that show how frictions arise in the absence of cash management. We use this model to estimate the economic value of cash management in SectionV.C. TheInternet Appendixprovides formal derivations of these markets in SectionVand an in-depth discussion of institutional details in SectionIV.
  Figure1depicts supply and demand in the wholesale market. In this market, suppliers sell marijuana to dispensaries at wholesale prices, and transactions consist of large quantities of marijuana and cash being exchanged. We model suppliers as having perfectly elastic supply (horizontal lines) and dispensaries as having imperfectly elastic demand (downward-sloping line).23Frictions in this market arise due to regulations that limit access to cash management. We model these frictions as an increase in the marginal cost and thus as an upward shift in the supply curve from “Supply with CM” to “Supply without CM.” SectionII.Bidentifies potential sources of these frictions.
  This model generates two testable predictions about the wholesale market. The first prediction is that dispensaries with cash management pay lower wholesale prices than dispensaries without cash management. In Figure1, this can be seen as the supply curve without frictions intersecting the demand curve at a lower price than the supply curve with frictions. The second prediction is that dispensaries with cash management buy more quantity than dispensaries without cash management. In Figure1, this is reflected as the supply curve without frictions intersecting the demand curve at a higher quantity than the implied quantity with frictions.
  Figure2depicts the retail market. In this market, customers buy marijuana from dispensaries at retail prices. We model customers as having perfectly elastic demand (horizontal lines) and dispensaries as having imperfectly elastic supply (upward-sloping line).24This market also faces frictions that arise from regulations that limit access to cash management. We model these frictions as lowering the willingness to pay for marijuana and thus as a downward shift in the demand curve from “Demand with CM” to “Demand without CM.” These frictions could be due to a lack of reputational capital, sometimes referred to as a “clientele effect” (Buchak et al. (2018)), which we discuss in the next section.
  The model leads to two testable predictions about the retail market. The first prediction is that dispensaries with cash management charge higher retail prices than dispensaries without cash management. Dispensaries with cash management charge a higher retail price because their demand curve is higher than that for dispensaries without cash management. As can be seen in Figure2, this higher demand leads the intersection of their demand and supply curves to be at a higher retail price than the intersection of the demand and supply curves for dispensaries without cash management. The second prediction on the retail side is that dispensaries with cash management sell larger quantities than dispensaries without cash management. As can be seen in Figure2, the higher demand for dispensaries with cash management leads the intersection of their demand and supply curves to be at a higher quantity than the intersection of the demand and supply curves for dispensaries without cash management.
  Figure1shows that suppliers charge lower wholesale prices and sell higher quantities to dispensaries with cash management. In the wholesale market, these effects are likely driven by both logistical considerations associated with cash transactions and uncertainty about the integrity of the dispensary-supplier relationship. Without cash management services, the physical transportation of cash increases risk, which has led to licensed transporters charging 1% to 1.5% of the value of cash-in-transit to cover insurance. Moreover, suppliers must count cash payments, which are often in small currency denominations, and safely store the cash.25Uncertainty arises in these relationships because suppliers bear the risk that dispensaries will be unable to pay or that they will pay with counterfeit currency. Finally, suppliers must continually produce marijuana but face uncertainty about future demand.
  Cash management resolves these concerns. Dispensaries with cash management can use credit unions and payment processing to mitigate the costs and risks of cash transactions. Moreover, dispensaries with cash management are able to develop a loyal customer base in the retail market through a clientele effect, which allows dispensaries to develop stable demand and accurate estimates for wholesale purchases. These dispensaries build reputational capital in the wholesale market, and wholesalers provide discounts and high-quality products to these dispensaries.
  Figure2depicts the retail market in which customers are willing to pay higher retail prices and demand higher quantities from dispensaries with cash management. Although the marijuana industry in Washington state is highly regulated, uncertainty about product quality cannot be resolved entirely before the customer purchases the product. When quality is unobservable and cannot be guaranteed, firms can offer an implicit quality guarantee through reputational capital (Klein and Leffler (1981) and Shapiro (1983)). Dispensaries that offer high-quality products can charge a premium price for their product to support the high-quality promise. Cash management services create quasi-rents that increase the value of the firm's reputational capital, creating a feedback loop whereby firms with cash management invest even more in reputational capital, which leads in turn to clientele effects.
  In our setting, clientele effects likely drive most of the higher retail prices of dispensaries with cash management, for several reasons. Most directly, customers report that they are willing to pay higher prices for clientele effects (Brasler (2014)).26Customer reviews posted to Yelp further reveal that customers expect consistency, quality, and safety in a dispensary's products and build close relationships with their budtenders.27In addition, because cash management creates an incentive to invest in reputational capital in the wholesale market, these dispensaries are able to buy consistently high-quality products in the wholesale market. This has ripple effects on the retail side of the business. Dispensaries are able to provide predictable product quality and safety, and can improve a customer's experience by hiring good budtenders and offering a fully stocked range of products. Hence, the clientele effect should provide a quality guarantee in the retail market that increases the marginal value to customers and increases demand for products from dispensaries with cash management relative to dispensaries without cash management.
article.III. Data:
We combine three novel data sets for our empirical analysis. First, we use administrative records from the WSLCB. Second, we compile data about financial institutions using data from the FDIC and the NCUA. Third, we use data based on our hand-collected surveys of marijuana business owners. We discuss each of these data sets in turn. We then provide descriptive statistics at the end of this section.
  States that have legalized recreational marijuana for adult use have imposed extensive data reporting requirements.28In Washington state, firms are required to report germination, harvest, production, and final retail sales data for each marijuana plant. Firms record the strain, quantity (weight), retail price, and wholesale price for each transaction. Firms have a large incentive to accurately report this information because misreporting has minimal benefits (e.g., it does not determine tax liability) but large potential costs, as firms are subject to audit with the risk of losing their license. For example, Washington state uses random audits of dispensaries and suppliers, and employs penalties and inventory destruction in the event of misreporting, to ensure that marijuana dispensaries comply with the regulations and disclose this information accurately in the tracking system.
  The WSLCB data set covers the universe of marijuana dispensaries and includes 62 million retail transactions from July 2014 to February 2017. Because the data are tracked at the transaction level, we are able to construct transaction-level variables. We define transaction-level profitability asProfitability=(retail price−wholesale price)×quantity${\it Profitability} = (\text{retail price} - \text{wholesale price})\times \text{quantity}$. We emphasize thatprofitabilityis a measure of product-level profits rather than firm-level profits and thus is a function only of the product that the firm sells less what it paid for the product. The measure does not include labor costs, building costs, or other fixed costs, and thus is not a measure of the overall efficiency of the firm.29
  In the administrative data set, the averageretail priceis $12.04 per gram and the average transaction size (sales) is 6.8 g. For comparison, the average tobacco cigarette weighs 1 g and costs $0.31. The data set includes information about the strains of marijuana, which can be thought of as wine varietals, such as cabernet sauvignon. The average dispensary sold 262 different strains, including Pineapple Kush, Super Lemon Haze, and Snoops Blue Dream. Figure3shows dispensary locations in Washington state using their latitude and longitude. Each county is shaded depending on the amount of sales. This figure shows that while there is a large volume of sales in and around Seattle, dispensaries and sales are spread across the entire state.30
  We combine the administrative data with the geographic locations of branches and headquarters of credit unions and banks in Washington state from the NCUA and the FDIC Summary of Deposits. In total, there are 1,481 branches of financial institutions and 52 banks, and 40 credit unions headquartered in Washington with at least one branch. On average, banks have 20 branches and credit unions have 6.4 branches.
  In June 2014, two independent credit unions—Salal and Numerica—elected to work with marijuana dispensaries following the passage of initiative 502.31We refer to these credit unions as 502 credit unions. Together these two credit unions have 21 branches. Figure4shows all credit union locations in the state. As can be seen, 502 and non-502 credit unions are evenly spaced across the state and in counties with high and low average dispensary profits.
  The characteristics of 502 credit unions and non-502 credit unions are similar along most dimensions. TableIA.IIprovides summary statistics based on NCUA Call Reports data. As can be seen, 502 and non-502 credit unions are similar in terms of assets, deposits, and net income. In addition, 502 and non-502 credit unions have similar bank outreach in terms of number of members, number of branches, and plans to expand branch networks, and proprietary data show that they have similar FICO scores, number of car loans, and other lending measures.32However, 502 credit unions have higher total loans and leases, as well as higher liabilities. These differences may make 502 credit unions more willing to risk working with marijuana dispensaries.
  Our third data set comes from a survey we conducted between July 10, 2016 to October 31, 2016, namely, the BERT study. We contacted all dispensaries in Colorado, Oregon, and Washington over several waves that included letters, phone calls, partnerships with industry groups, and a field team. Participants were asked to take an online survey that took an average of 40 minutes. We followed up with phone calls in several additional waves with a shorter targeted survey. All participants were compensated $50.
  After all waves of the survey, the survey response rate was 21%. This response rate is a bit higher than in other recent surveys of businesses, likely because we applied a more intensive recruitment strategy. For example, Graham and Harvey (2001) obtained a response rate of 16% and Trahan and Gitman (1995) a response rate of 12% in surveys of CFOs and business executives of large firms.
  The WSLCB administrative data include 314 organizations that own 354 dispensaries, after restricting attention to locations that have sales (Mace, Patel, and Seegert (2020)). Most organizations in our sample have only one location; they were restricted by law to having no more than three. We supplemented the survey from the BERT study with additional waves of a shorter survey to ensure we captured responses from all 314 organizations in Washington state. Specifically, we obtain responses from all organizations in the state on whether they have access to cash management. The survey also elicited demographic information on the owners, including education level and whether their parents had run a business (Parent run company) as a proxy for exposure to entrepreneurship. We received responses to demographic questions from 238 organizations (76%), which corresponds to 264 dispensaries (75%). We assign responses to all locations of an organization. We also assume that organizations that report having access to cash management continue to have access throughout the sample period, while those that report not having access do not obtain access at any point in our sample period. This assumption is a limitation of a one-time survey and likely leads to measurement error. Because such measurement error is expected to bias OLS estimates toward zero, we focus on IV evidence in our main analysis (Hausman et al. (1991), Bound, Brown, and Mathiowetz (2001), and Schennach (2007)). SectionII.Dof theInternet Appendixreports OLS estimates and provides a comparison of estimates.
  TableIIIpresents descriptive statistics. Column (1) reports full-sample averages, column (2) reports averages for dispensaries located within one mile of a 502 credit union, column (3) reports averages for dispensaries located 1 to 10 miles from a 502 credit union, and column (4) reports differences and their statistical significance.
  Panel A shows economically and statistically meaningful differences in operation-specific variables between dispensaries within one mile of a 502 credit union (column (2)) and dispensaries between 1 and 10 miles of a 502 credit union (column (3)). In particular, retail prices, wholesale prices, profitability, and sales are all larger for dispensaries within one mile of a 502 credit union than for those located 1 to 10 miles away from a 502 credit union.
  Panel B reports summary statistics for owner characteristics such as educational attainment and whether the owner's parent had run a company (Parent run company), which is a proxy for a dispensary operator's exposure to entrepreneurship. We find that these characteristics are economically and statistically similar for dispensaries in columns (2) and (3).33These results suggest a limited role for business acumen of the owner or the strategic choice of location to explain our results.
  Panel C summarizes the average distances between dispensaries and 502 credit unions, non-502 credit unions, banks, and competitor dispensaries. Dispensaries in columns (2) and (3) have similar distances to the latter three types of establishments. Specifically, the average difference in the distance to a non-502 credit union (Miles to non-502 credit union), bank (Miles to bank), and competitor (Miles to competitor) is 0.06, 0.3, and 0.11 miles, respectively. Our empirical design allows us to capture the possibility that dispensaries, non-502 credit unions, 502 credit unions, and banks locate near economic opportunities by comparing the profitability of dispensaries near economic opportunities with and without 502 credit unions. We also include the distance to the nearest bank as an indirect control for local economic opportunities, and we conduct placebo tests using distance to a bank, rather than a 502 credit union, as a placebo instrument (see TableIA.Xand Figure7).
article.IV. Design:
Our goal is to measure the causal effects of cash management on dispensary profitability. Ideally, we would examine an experimental setting in which cash management is assigned randomly to some dispensaries and not to others, mitigating concerns that dispensaries with cash management differ from dispensaries without cash management. In the absence of a truly random experiment, we use an IV approach to address this potential bias.
  Our instrument is based on the distance between a marijuana dispensary and a 502 credit union, which for several institutional reasons is likely to be random. Distance is often used as an instrument for causal identification in the literature (Card (1995), Currie and Moretti (2003), and Cameron and Taber (2004)). In our setting of marijuana banking, distance is likely a particularly strong instrument due to the extra monitoring, including quarterly site visits from bank personnel, that strengthen the relationship between distance, information asymmetry, and cash management (e.g., Berger et al. (2005)). Our statistical tests discussed below support this view and show that distance is a good predictor of cash management services in the marijuana industry.
  In the first-stage equation (equation (1)), the outcome variable,Cash management, is an indicator equal to one for marijuana dispensaries that have access to cash management based on our survey responses, and zero otherwise. The distance measure,Distance to 502 credit unionis constructed in two ways. First, we use a discrete measure of distance, where f(Distance to 502 credit union) is an indicator equal to one if a marijuana dispensary is within one mile of a 502 credit union and zero otherwise. Second, we use a continuous measure, log(Distance to nearest 502 credit union). The results are not sensitive to the definitions that we use to construct these measures or to using different distance thresholds for the discrete measure, as we discuss in SectionIV.B.
  In the second-stage equation (equation (2)), log(Profitability) is the dependent variable. It is measured at the dispensary-strain level at a weekly frequency and is measured after the random assignment of access to cash management. We control for product market competition by including the presence of other dispensaries within one mile (Competitors). The specification includes week (αt$\alpha _t$) and city (αj$\alpha _j$) fixed effects, and standard errors are clustered at the dispensary level. The coefficient of interest isβ1$\beta _1$, the average profitability across marijuana dispensaries that have cash management because they are close to a 502 credit union.
  We find a strong correlation between cash management and a marijuana dispensary's distance to a 502 credit union, as modeled by equation (1). TableIVshows that marijuana dispensaries within one mile of a 502 credit union have a 28 percentage point higher probability of having cash management services than those farther than one mile (column (1)). The probability of having cash management decreases monotonically as the discrete measure increases from within one mile to within three miles (columns (1) to (3)). The results in column (4) suggest that a 10% increase in a marijuana dispensary's distance from a 502 credit union (log(Distance to nearest 502 credit union)) decreases the probability of access to cash management by 1 percentage point. TheF-statistic for most of these specifications is above the cutoff for a strong instrument (Bound, Jaeger, and Baker (1995) and Stock, Wright, and Yogo (2002)). Notwithstanding, as a precaution we run several placebo tests using the distance to banks, non-502 credit unions, and other types of financial institutions. We find no evidence that distance to these alternative financial institutions predicts access to cash management (see TableIA.Vand SectionIIof theInternet Appendixfor a detailed discussion).
  For distance to be a valid instrument, the assignment of distance must be uncorrelated with marijuana dispensary characteristics. This random assignment mechanism is sufficient for consistent estimation of the reduced-form effects of distance on access to cash management. Two further assumptions are necessary, however, to interpret the IV estimates as causal effects: (i) monotonicity and (ii) an exclusion restriction. We briefly discuss these in turn, leaving a more detailed discussion for SectionIIof theInternet Appendix.
  The monotonicity assumption in our setting requires that being closer to a 502 credit union does not decrease dispensaries' probability of access to cash management. This assumption is likely to hold in our setting, as only two credit unions work with dispensaries and the relationship between dispensary and credit union entails physical costs. We also find that the correlation betweenDistance to the nearest 502 credit unionandCash managementis consistently positive and sizable in subsamples of the data based on pre-determined characteristics (see TableIA.III).
  Our exclusion restriction is that distance affects marijuana dispensary profits only through its impact on access to cash management. The exclusion restriction is likely to hold in our setting because of the asynchronous timing of the location decisions by dispensaries and the decision to provide banking services to marijuana businesses by credit unions. Figure5depicts the timeline of these decisions and demonstrates that it would have been difficult for either group to make its decision strategically based on the decision of the other group. In particular, potential marijuana dispensaries made location decisions when they applied for a license (December 2013). This step occurred before any credit union elected to offer banking services to the marijuana industry, making it impossible for the potential dispensaries to choose locations based on credit unions that would work with them. Moreover, only a random subset of these dispensaries would ultimately receive a license. Lottery winners were announced in May 2014 but could not start transacting with customers until July 2014. Two credit unions announced their intention to work with the marijuana industry in June 2014. This decision occurred well before any dispensary was open for business, making it impossible for credit unions to make their decision based on a dispensary's profitability. Due to this timing, it is likely that credit unions did not know where licensed dispensaries would be located, but if they did have it this knowledge would not be a violation of the exclusion restriction—a violation would be obtained only if they knew the profitability of dispensaries, which could not be known before July 2014 when dispensaries opened. It would also be difficult for credit unions to colocate with profitable dispensaries due to the number of branches that they have. Put differently, even if a profitable dispensary was located near one of their branches, there was no guarantee that profitable dispensaries would be located near their other branches. Figure4further shows that the 502 credit unions are spread throughout Washington state, reducing the concern that credit unions locate only in the most lucrative areas. These institutional details make it likely that the distance between a marijuana dispensary and a 502 credit union is exogenous. In SectionIIIof theInternet Appendix, we discuss a series of tests that we employ to check alternative mechanisms and other potential threats to validity.34
article.V. Results:
We report our estimates of the impact of cash management on profitability in SectionV.A. We explore alternative explanations and the sensitivity of our results in SectionV.B. We investigate mechanisms driving the results in SectionV.C. Additional tests are reported in SectionIIIof theInternet Appendix.
  Figure6presents reduced-form evidence of our IV strategy by plotting the relationship between profitability and distance (Miles from Financial Institution) in the raw data. The figure reports the marijuana dispensary profitability of individual marijuana strains by distance to a financial institution—Distance to bank(dotted line),Distance to non-502 credit union(dashed line), andDistance to 502 credit union(solid line)—where distance is predictive of access to cash management (see SectionIV.B). Working backward from a distance of 10 miles, the figure shows that the lines are all parallel and flat in terms of profitability, meaning that profitability is similar across all financial institutions at these distances. Starting at three miles, log(Profitability) increases as theDistance to 502 credit uniondeclines. For dispensaries within one mile of a 502 credit union, log(Profitability) is 10% higher than for dispensaries within one mile of banks or non-502 credit unions.
  We use our quasi-experimental IV setting to test for a causal relationship between access to cash management and marijuana dispensary profitability. In TableV, we show that product profitability (at the strain level) is higher at dispensaries with cash management. In column (1), we present our most parsimonious model, which includes the discrete measure of distance as the IV, no control variables, and fixed effects only for strain. In this model, we find that cash management increases product profitability by 37.8%. When we include week fixed effects in column (2), the precision of the estimate increases and the point estimate remains similar at 39.5%.
  Our results are robust to a series of potential confounding factors. One concern might be that highly profitable dispensaries cluster in Seattle and Spokane (alongside the 502 credit unions), which drives the results. To control for this possibility, we include city fixed effects in the model reported in column (3) and find a slightly higher effect of 52.8%.35This estimate relies on within-city variation, meaning that the estimate is not a result of differences in profitability across cities. Another concern might be that highly profitable dispensaries cluster near areas with high economic activity that also include banks. To control for these local conditions, we control forDistance to nearest bankin columns (4) to (8). Finally, we control for the extent of competition using the number of other dispensaries (Competitors) in columns (5) to (8).36
  In a related test, we restrict our sample to generic strains and house varietals to investigate whether brand quality may be an omitted variable related to microlocations or clientele. Column (6) shows that the estimates for this subset are very similar to those for the full sample of 51.7%.
  As we mention in the introduction, our profitability measure does not include labor costs and other fixed costs such as rent and utilities, and hence it should not be compared with firm profits. Bank fees, cash storage, and secure cash transportation expenses also do not enter our estimates. Rather, profitability only captures cash flows from retail prices, wholesale prices, and sales quantities (grams).
  We report our most saturated model in columns (5) and (7). These specifications include strain, week, and city fixed effects as well as controls for local economic conditions (i.e.,Distance to bank) and competition from other local dispensaries (Competitors). The estimated effect ofCash managementonProfitabilityis 48.5% using the indicator instrument (column (5)) and 45.6% using the continuous distance instrument (log(Distance to 502 credit union)).
  Standard errors are clustered at the dispensary level. However, in this setting, there is no ideal level of clustering, and there are good arguments for clustering at other levels, such as the week and city level; see SectionIIIof theInternet Appendixfor a more detailed discussion. We address this ambiguity by estimating our main effects using standard errors clustered at alternative and perhaps equally valid levels. We also aggregate data at the daily, weekly, and monthly frequencies to explore how aggregation influences our results. Our estimates remain statistically significant using different levels of clustering, using two-way clustering at the week-dispensary level (see FigureIA.1), and using different aggregation frequencies (see Figure8).
  In column (8), we report results of a dispensary level, rather than strain-level, analysis. We aggregate the data to the dispensary-week level and include city and week fixed effects as well as controls for the distance to the nearest bank and competition. Dispensary profitability is 23.8% higher when a dispensary has access to cash management. Our results therefore show that cash management has a large impact on a dispensary's profitability. In the remainder of the paper, we examine how regulations that limit access to cash management shift supply and demand in the wholesale and retail markets and the implications for prices and quantities.
  We use a series of placebo tests to investigate alternative explanations. We useDistance to non-502 credit unionandDistance to nearest bankas placebo instruments. Estimates from these placebo tests are reported in Figure7and capture unobserved characteristics associated with distance and profitability that could conflate our estimates. SectionIIIof theInternet Appendixreports estimates of placebo tests in which we find that the distance between a dispensary and a non-502 credit union or bank does not impact product profitability.
  These estimates help to rule out alternative explanations whereby the effect of the distance to a 502 credit union on product profitability operates through the types of locations where financial institutions are found (such as shopping areas) rather than through cash management.
  We report results of 11 additional specifications in Figure8to explore the sensitivity of our estimates to the various modeling choices in our main specification. Our baseline estimate excludes marijuana dispensaries that are farther than 10 miles from a 502 credit union. In alternative specifications, we expand that restriction to 15 miles, reduce the restriction to five miles, and eliminate the restriction. We next explore the effect of the choice of sample period on the estimates by only including transactions from 2015 to 2017. We also use an alternative control for competition, and replace the baseline instrument (an indicator for being within one mile of a 502 credit union) with indicators for being within two or three miles of a 502 credit union or with the continuous measure log(Distance to 502 credit union). We further assess the sensitivity of our results to the level of aggregation in our data, considering monthly and daily aggregation rather than weekly. The estimates remain similar and statistically significant in all specifications. As expected, confidence intervals change as models become more or less restrictive. See SectionIIIof theInternet Appendixfor additional discussion and test results.
  We next use our IV model to examine how cash management affects the components of profitability: wholesale price, retail price, and sales volume. We estimate a series of regression specifications in which a marijuana dispensary's access to cash management is the primary explanatory variable.37
  The predictions from our conceptual framework are that firms with access to cash management will pay lower wholesale prices, charge higher retail prices, and sell larger quantities (sales). In addition to these dependent variables, we consider whether cash management affects the number of strains sold. We report specifications with and without strain fixed effects to isolate product-level price variation across dispensaries from dispensary-level price variation across all products. In other words, we isolate the prices required to create reputational capital in the wholesale market and clientele effects in the retail market. The specifications with strain fixed effects show the pricing power of dispensaries with and without cash management for the same product. Comparing these results to specifications without strain fixed effects reveals how differences in a dispensary's product offerings (e.g., variety, quality, and uniqueness) affect prices and sales for dispensaries with and without cash management. We expect differences in product bundles if cash management allows dispensaries to build reputational capital with suppliers (wholesale market) and to cater to customers with greater demand for quality assurance (retail market).
  We first consider the difference in wholesale prices with strain fixed effects, which we report in TableVI, column (2). Dispensaries with cash management pay suppliers 7.3% less for the same strain compared to dispensaries without cash management (column (2)). Specifically, compared to the average wholesale price of $6.93 per gram (see TableIII), dispensaries with cash management pay $6.42 per gram, or $0.51 less. This difference in wholesale price reflects the value of a dispensary's reputational capital in the wholesale market. As discussed in SectionII, cash management reduces transaction costs and uncertainty, leading suppliers to sell at a discounted price to high-quality dispensaries, that is, dispensaries with cash management (see Figure1).
  We next consider the difference in retail prices with strain fixed effects, which we report in column (4) of TableVI. Dispensaries with cash management charge customers 8.3% more for the same product (strain) as dispensaries without cash management. Specifically, customers pay an average retail price of $13.04 at these dispensaries versus $12.04 in dispensaries without cash management—a difference of $1.00—for the same product. This difference reflects the price premium necessary to ensure that the dispensary provides high-quality products. Cash management allows dispensaries to secure consistently high-quality products in the wholesale market through reputational capital. Carrying high-quality products has ripple effects in the retail market through a clientele effect, which has been shown to be valuable in other markets (Buchak et al. (2018)). For example, carrying high-quality products can allow dispensaries to hire better budtenders who can spend more time with customers discussing the product—a main benefit cited in Yelp reviews. See SectionIIfor a detailed discussion of these costs and benefits.
  Turning to the difference in quantities sold (sales) with strain fixed effects, reported in column (6) of TableVI, we find that dispensaries with cash management sell 19.0% more of the same product (strain) relative to dispensaries without cash management. This difference suggests that dispensaries with cash management sell 1.29 (= 8.09 − 6.8) more grams per transaction.
  We use the estimates in TableVIto quantify the contribution of each factor to the total increase in profitability for dispensaries with cash management. We find that dispensaries with cash management earn $8.09 (=($13.04 − $12.04)×$\times$8.09) more per transaction as a result of being able to charge higher retail prices. Lower wholesale prices lead to an additional $4.13 (= ($6.93 − $6.42)×$\times$8.09) for dispensaries with cash management, while selling large quantities leads dispensaries with cash management to earn $6.59 (= ($12.04 − $6.93)×$\times$(8.09 - 6.8)) more per transaction. Finally, we find that selling a higher-quality bundle of goods leads to $2.25 greater earnings.38In sum, we find that dispensaries with cash management sell 9% more strains (column (7) of TableVI) and sell higher-quality product as indicated by higher wholesale prices and retail prices (columns (1) to (4) of TableVI). Taken together, we find that dispensaries with cash management earn $21.06 more per transaction than dispensaries without cash management.
  SectionVof theInternet Appendixcombines the model from SectionIIand our estimates of the differences in prices and quantities to calculate the total economic value of cash management in the marijuana industry in Washington state over the July 2014 to January 2017 period if all dispensaries had access to cash management. We find that the total economic value of cash management is $17,683,448. We obtain this value by multiplying the transaction values by the total number of observations. This value, of which $5,973,076 comes from the wholesale market and $11,710,372 from the retail market, amounts to about 1.8% of total industry sales during our sample period across our full sample if all dispensaries had cash management (Pellicciotti (2022)).39
article.VI. Discussion:
In this section, we discuss the alternative empirical approaches that we employ to test the robustness of our results, rule out alternative explanations, and deepen our understanding of the link between cash management and firm profitability.
  In SectionsIIandIIIof theInternet Appendix, we explore the robustness of our results using several different empirical approaches to assess how confounding factors alter the conclusions from our IV strategy. First, we assess how confounding factors, such as an owner's business acumen, that affect both access to cash management and business success affect a simple OLS analysis. A detailed discussion in SectionII.Din theInternet Appendixshows that the OLS estimates are consistent with our IV results, but the difference between these estimates suggests that confounding factors bias the OLS results.
  Next, we use a differences-in-differences analysis to test whether unobserved differences across neighborhoods can explain our results. In this analysis, which we report in SectionIII.Aof theInternet Appendix, we use discrete measures of distance to proxy for access to cash management in an OLS setting. We explore how factors such as urban versus rural geographies and “main street” agglomeration affect our IV results. The estimates bolster our IV findings suggesting that profitability is higher only for those dispensaries close to a 502 credit union and highlight the strength of using distance as an exogenous variable.
  Finally, SectionIII.Bof theInternet Appendixreports regression estimates based on a county-level IV that uses a credit union's banking market to define access to cash management. In particular, the instrument measures whether a dispensary is located in a county with a 502 credit union. Under this definition, a dispensary has access to cash management if it is within a credit union's banking market rather than within a fixed, linear distance. We find that counties with and without 502 credit unions are indistinguishable in terms of observable demographic and economic characteristics. The results of this analysis complement our main results by confirming that profitability is higher in counties with a 502 credit union.
  In sum, these findings allow us to rule out many alternative explanations for our results. In addition, the similarity in results across methods and instruments suggests that our estimates are not due to a particular set of identifying assumptions.
  By increasing profitability, cash management may also affect the extensive margin by encouraging new entrants. Typically, profits and losses drive entry and exit in a frictionless product market. The marijuana industry, however, is far from frictionless.
  The lottery for licenses and the extensive regulatory approvals required for licensed dispensaries to start doing business create frictions that substantially reduce the effects that action on the extensive margin would have on marginal profitability.40
  We examine entry and exit decisions throughout our sample and find that entry was gradual over the 2014 to 2017 period, while there were only two exits over the same period. Specifically, 321 licenses were granted and retail dispensary storefronts opened. The number of entries was spread evenly throughout the time series, which suggests that compliance with the time-consuming and onerous licensing process determined entry, rather than changes in product market competition. In SectionIVof theInternet Appendix, we further show that new entry is not correlated with proximity to a credit union or with profitability following entry. Due to these features of the market, we focus on the intensive-margin effects—changes in prices and quantities in the wholesale and retail markets.
article.VII. Conclusion:
Cash management services provided by financial institutions produce substantial economic value for firms. These services include safe storage, verification, and guarantee of funds. When firms lack these services, they must find alternatives and provide internal warehousing of cash that adds costs to transactions and diverts resources from the core business. In the marijuana industry, cash management services help dispensaries build reputational capital and cater to clientele who value a premium experience. We show that these costs shift supply and demand in the wholesale and retail markets. The value of access to cash management services comes from paying lower wholesale prices, charging higher retail prices, and selling larger quantities than firms without these services.
  Cash management services become an essential part of a firm's operation, much like water or electricity. However, the value of cash management is difficult to quantify because its ubiquitous adoption provides little variation to analyze. We focus on the legal marijuana industry over the 2014 to 2017 period to overcome the empirical challenges endemic to studying the value of cash management. Our empirical setting represents a unique point in time when the confluence of financial regulations, industry regulations, and an unprecedented level of tracking and traceability in a product market provide both rich data and plausibly random variation in the adoption of cash management services.
  We find that dispensaries with cash management charge higher retail prices ($1 more per gram), pay lower wholesale prices ($0.51 per gram), and buy and sell larger quantities (19%), consistent with the predictions of our model. Taken together, these effects suggest that dispensaries with cash management earn $21.06 more per transaction than dispensaries without cash management (= $55.81 − $34.75), which amounts to a 40% increase in profitability.
  Although our results are rooted in the heavily regulated, cash-intensive marijuana industry, the fundamental benefits of warehousing (storage, security, verification, and issuance of currency) extend beyond our setting and beyond the management of physical cash. Without warehousing, firms that use digital banking, cryptocurrencies, and fintech solutions must bear the costs of developing infrastructure to serve these functions. Our model suggests that the benefits of warehousing exist regardless of whether national banks, credit unions, or fintech provide these warehousing functions.
  Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong
